name: DeepSeek-V4-Pro (Think Max)
archDisplay: Mixture-of-Experts (1.6T total / 49B active), hybrid Compressed Sparse Attention + Heavily Compressed Attention, Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections, Muon optimizer
note: Fast/low-latency mode of the DeepSeek-V4-Pro preview checkpoint, the default reasoning tier a user gets without selecting anything. (maximum reasoning-effort tier / 'Pro-Max' mode, DeepSeek's most capable released configuration).

results.notes: BrowseComp Pass@1, Think Max / Pro-Max mode. Cross-verified identical value in DeepSeek-V4 tech report Table 6/7 (arXiv:2606.19348).
GDPval-AA Elo, Think Max / Pro-Max mode. Only reported for the Max reasoning tier. Cross-verified identical value in DeepSeek-V4 tech report Table 6/7 (arXiv:2606.19348).

ARC-AGI-2 semi-private benchmark, 46%, from NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) independent evaluation of DeepSeek V4 Pro (published 2026-05-01, updated 2026-05-02). CAISI's methodology used 'developer-recommended settings ... preserving internal reasoning ... and maximum thinking' -- i.e. the Think Max / Pro-Max tier, matching this catalog entry. CAISI reproduced DeepSeek's self-reported GPQA-Diamond score (~90%, matching the value already in the corpus) to validate its test harness before running additional held-out benchmarks including this ARC-AGI-2 semi-private set, which does not appear in DeepSeek's own model card or tech report. Cross-checked against a secondary summary (theweatherreport.ai) reporting the same 46% figure. Note: CAISI's report also gives an independent SWE-bench Verified figure (74%) and GPQA-Diamond (~90%), but both benchmark slugs are already tracked for this model from DeepSeek's self-reported numbers, so per instructions those were not added as new entries.

SealedBench sealed capability index — composite of capability, token efficiency, speed and cost; rank 17/28 on the 2026-07-22 run (sealedbench.com). Listed as "DeepSeek V4 Pro" (DeepSeek). Evaluated at the highest available thinking setting. Attached to the Think Max effort sibling of DeepSeek-V4-Pro (highest available thinking).

verificationNotes:
Confirmed real via DeepSeek's own official API-docs news post (api-docs.deepseek.com/news/news260424/, dated 2026-04-24) announcing the V4-Pro/V4-Flash preview, and via the deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro Hugging Face model card, which gives 1.6T total/49B active params, MIT license, 1M-token context, and a benchmark table covering three reasoning tiers (Non-Think/Think High/Think Max). Numerous SEO/content-farm sites (codersera, sitepoint, morphllm, etc.) published speculative-sounding 'release timeline' articles with suspiciously precise but unverifiable claims (e.g. percentile rankings, an 'Intelligence Index' score) — those were NOT used; all numbers here come from the official HF model card table.

2026-07-16: Checked all five missing core benchmarks (mmlu, math, aime, humaneval, mmmu) specifically for the Think Max / Pro-Max reasoning tier. DeepSeek's own HF README (huggingface.co/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro) contains two distinct benchmark tables: a 'Base Model Evaluation Results' table -- which does report MMLU 90.1 (5-shot EM), MATH 64.5 (4-shot EM), and HumanEval 76.8 (Pass@1, 0-shot) -- but explicitly for the pre-RL 'DeepSeek-V4-Pro-Base' pretraining checkpoint, a variant this corpus does not track as its own model entry; and a separate 'Reasoning Mode Comparison' table covering the three released instruct tiers this corpus does track (Non-Think / Think-High / Think-Max). That second table has no plain-MMLU, MATH, AIME, or HumanEval row for any tier -- DeepSeek substituted MMLU-Pro (already in corpus), HMMT 2026 Feb, and IMOAnswerBench for the math/knowledge slots, and LiveCodeBench (already in corpus) for the coding slot. Attributing the base-checkpoint MMLU/MATH/HumanEval numbers to the Think-Max tier would misattribute a different, untuned checkpoint's score to this RL-tuned configuration, so they were not added. Cross-checked arXiv:2606.19348 (DeepSeek-V4 tech report) -- no AIME or MMMU rows appear anywhere in the paper for any tier. Checked Vals.ai (vals.ai/models/deepseek_deepseek-v4-pro) and Epoch AI's public benchmarking hub -- neither independently tracks plain MMLU, MATH, AIME, or HumanEval for this model (Vals.ai's tracked set is GPQA Diamond / LiveCodeBench / MMLU-Pro / SWE-bench / Terminal-Bench, none of which are new). Cross-referenced all five sibling DeepSeek-V4 family files (Flash and Pro, all three tiers) -- none has mmlu, math, aime, humaneval, or mmmu recorded either, confirming a consistent, deliberate absence across the whole release rather than a per-file gap. MMMU additionally does not apply on capability grounds: this model's capabilities.vision is false (text-only), consistent with DeepSeek publishing no vision benchmark for any DeepSeek-V4 tier. All five gaps resolved as omitted_no_evidence; no existing entries were altered.

2026-07-17 (round-2 deep pass, new source types only -- HELM, arXiv/Semantic Scholar third-party citations, GitHub eval-harness logs, non-DeepSeek leaderboards/aggregators, non-English sources, sibling/competitor comparison tables): Re-chased mmlu, math, aime, humaneval using source types NOT covered by the 2026-07-16 pass. All 6 recommended new source types were checked; none produced a credible, tier-matched score.

1. Stanford HELM: Queried crfm.stanford.edu/helm/ (root), /helm/mmlu/latest/, and /helm/capabilities/latest/ directly; the site is a client-rendered SPA so WebFetch could not extract the live table, but search-engine summarization of the same pages independently corroborates that DeepSeek v3 (not v4) is HELM Capabilities' top-ranked open-weight model with mean score 0.665 -- no DeepSeek-V4-Pro entry is indexed anywhere on HELM. Also checked github.com/stanford-crfm/helm (README, CHANGELOG, releases) for any V4 run additions -- none found. HELM has not evaluated this model.

2. arXiv / Semantic Scholar third-party baselines: Re-fetched the full HTML of the DeepSeek-V4 tech report itself (arxiv.org/html/2606.19348v1) hunting specifically for MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval table rows across all tiers -- consistent with the prior pass's finding, none exist for the reasoning-tier tables (only MMLU-Pro, HMMT, IMOAnswerBench, LiveCodeBench are reported for Think-Max). Broadly searched arXiv/Semantic Scholar for any *other* paper using DeepSeek-V4-Pro as a baseline/comparison point reporting these four benchmarks (Semantic Scholar Graph API itself returned HTTP 429 on direct query, so this relied on web-search cross-referencing of arXiv). Found only DeepSeek's own paper and one derivative long-context paper (FlashMemory-DeepSeek-V4, arXiv:2606.09079) which reports LongBench-v2/LongMemEval/RULER numbers, not MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval. No qualifying third-party paper found.

3. GitHub eval-harness run logs: Found and cloned a real, concrete example: github.com/harrrshall/tinyrouter (a TRINITY-replication project, arXiv:2512.04695, that calls `deepseek-v4-pro` live via the Fireworks AI API). Its committed results contain genuine per-model scores: experiments/final/mmlu_rigorous.json gives `single::deepseek-v4-pro = 0.9222` (mean of 3 reps, std 0.0104) on a 120-question held-out MMLU-style subset, and experiments/final/math_rigorous.json gives `single::deepseek-v4-pro = 0.7472` on MATH-500. However, per the repo's own configs/models.yaml and docs/JOURNAL.md entry "2026-06-22 -- Fireworks reasoning-effort mapping resolved" ("map 'minimal' -> reasoning_effort: 'low' in FireworksPool.chat"), every pool model including deepseek-v4-pro was called at **minimal/low reasoning effort** for this eval -- the opposite tier from the Think Max / maximum-reasoning-effort configuration this corpus file tracks. The MMLU figure is also a custom 120-question subset (not the standard ~14k-question test set) and the MATH figure is the MATH-500 curated subset, not the full MATH benchmark. Attributing either number to Think-Max would misattribute a different reasoning-effort configuration's score to this entry -- the same category of error the 2026-07-16 pass correctly avoided with the HF base-checkpoint table. Not added; repo was deleted from local scratch after inspection.

4. Other leaderboards/aggregators: LiveBench.ai is JS-rendered and its table could not be confirmed present or absent via WebFetch; no independent citation of a LiveBench score for this model surfaced via search. Scale AI's SEAL leaderboard (labs.scale.com/leaderboard) has no DeepSeek V4 entry at all, per corroborating search results. OpenRouter's model page and compare pages for deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro list only pricing and the benchmarks already in this corpus (GPQA, HLE, SWE-bench-Pro, LiveCodeBench) -- no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval. PapersWithCode searches for MMLU/HumanEval leaderboards returned no dedicated entry for this model (search results redirected to third-party aggregator sites like BenchLM instead of PapersWithCode itself). DataLearnerAI (a Chinese curated-leaderboard aggregator) confirmed via search snippet to track only "MMLU Pro (87.50, rank 11/126)", LiveCodeBench, and SWE-bench Verified for DeepSeek-V4-Pro -- no plain MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval row exists there either; direct WebFetch of datalearner.com failed (DNS resolution error in this sandbox) so this relied on search-snippet corroboration only.

5. Non-English-language sources: Checked Chinese community sources (zhihu.com, csdn blogs, 302.ai benchmark lab, ai-insight.org "评测榜单深度档案", datalearner.com). 302.ai (302.ai/blog/302-ai-benchmark-lab-review-on-deepseek-v4-pro) ran its own independent test but used custom scenario questions (10 logic/math, 7 "human intuition", 12 programming-simulation problems) rather than standard MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval, and did not specify a reasoning-tier setting. ai-insight.org's report states AIME 2025/2026 are industry-wide "saturated" at 95-99% but does not attribute a specific number to V4-Pro in the visible (non-paywalled) preview. Found one genuinely independent AIME 2026 evaluation (layerlens.ai/blog/deepseek-v4-flash-aime2026, by "Stratix") reporting 96.7% accuracy, "2nd of 14 models" -- but this is explicitly for DeepSeek V4 **Flash**, a sibling model, not V4-Pro; the page states it evaluated Flash only.

6. Sibling/competitor comparison tables: Checked GLM-5.2, Kimi K2, and Qwen3.5-era launch/comparison material (codingfleet.com, artificialanalysis.ai comparison pages, openrouter.ai compare pages) for any table listing DeepSeek-V4-Pro (Think Max) alongside MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval. Found comparison tables covering SWE-bench Pro, GPQA Diamond, and HLE (all benchmarks already in this corpus entry) but none reporting plain MMLU, MATH, AIME, or HumanEval for DeepSeek V4 Pro specifically -- competitor launch materials cite their own MMLU/AIME numbers but simply omit a DeepSeek-V4-Pro comparison column for those particular rows.

Conclusion: all 4 gaps (mmlu, math, aime, humaneval) remain unresolved after checking all 6 newly-specified source types (exceeding the 3-type minimum). No credible, tier-matched (Think Max / Pro-Max) independent score exists in any of them as of 2026-07-17. Re-affirmed as omitted_no_evidence; no existing entries altered.

2026-07-18 (round-3 final pass, NEW source types only -- Wayback Machine, conference talks/keynotes, investor/press materials, GitHub issues/SDK-repo discussions, third-party technical-blog analysis): Re-chased mmlu, math, aime, humaneval one last time using exactly the 5 source types specified for this round, none overlapping rounds 1-2 (official launch materials/Epoch/Vals.ai/LMArena/BenchLM/llm-stats.com; HELM/arXiv/GitHub-eval-logs/MathArena/LiveBench/OpenRouter/Scale-SEAL/regional-language/sibling-tables). All 5 were genuinely attempted.

1. Wayback Machine: Attempted to fetch archived snapshots of the official launch post (api-docs.deepseek.com/news/news260424/) and the HF model card via web.archive.org's CDX API and direct wayback URLs, plus archive.ph and the Memento aggregator (timetravel.mementoweb.org) as fallbacks -- all three were unreachable from this sandbox (WebFetch returned 'unable to fetch from web.archive.org' / 'unable to fetch from archive.ph' / DNS resolution failure for mementoweb.org). This is a tooling/network limitation, not a confirmed absence-of-evidence; genuinely attempted via three different archive access paths, none succeeded technically. No archived-snapshot evidence obtained either way.

2. Conference talks / keynote transcripts: Searched for DeepSeek V4 launch keynote, developer-conference talk, or podcast appearance referencing MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval. Found no dedicated keynote, conference talk, or podcast transcript for this model at all (DeepSeek does not appear to have done a live launch keynote for V4, consistent with its usual quiet-release pattern noted elsewhere in this corpus's verification history) -- only text blog posts/press coverage, already covered by other categories.

3. Investor / press materials: DeepSeek itself is a private company with no public earnings calls or investor decks of its own. Checked the closest available proxy -- Huawei's press/investor commentary on Ascend chip revenue tied to DeepSeek V4's 'day-zero' support (thedeepdive.ca, huaweicentral.com, 36kr.com coverage of Huawei chip-revenue growth attributed partly to DeepSeek V4 demand) -- this covers hardware/revenue figures only, no model-quality benchmark numbers. Also checked NVIDIA's investor-adjacent developer blog (developer.nvidia.com/blog/build-with-deepseek-v4-using-nvidia-blackwell-and-gpu-accelerated-endpoints) and NVIDIA's build.nvidia.com model card -- the developer blog confirms no MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval citation (only throughput/perf-per-watt figures); the model card page timed out and could not be loaded. No qualifying investor/press citation of these four benchmarks found anywhere.

4. GitHub issues / SDK repo discussions: Searched github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3 (V4's issues are filed against this repo since there is no separate DeepSeek-V4 repo) plus the broader GitHub issue/discussion corpus via `gh search issues` for combinations of "DeepSeek-V4-Pro"/"deepseek-v4" with MMLU, MATH, AIME, and HumanEval. Found only unrelated tooling bugs (e.g. issue #1244, a tool-call-formatting bug; vllm/sglang Day-0-support and kernel issues) and generic news-aggregation/daily-digest bot issues that summarize the model release without an official engineer reply containing benchmark figures. No GitHub issue or discussion thread contains an official reply with a real MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval number for this model.

5. Third-party review/analysis sites not yet checked this session: Directly fetched Simon Willison's launch-day post (simonwillison.net/2026/Apr/24/deepseek-v4/) -- discusses pricing/positioning only, no benchmark table reproduced. Searched and confirmed interconnects.ai's "Latest open artifacts (#21)" post discusses V4 Pro vs Flash positioning qualitatively ("Pro seems to underdeliver relative to its size") without citing MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval numbers. Directly fetched SemiAnalysis's InferenceX evaluation page (inferencex.semianalysis.com/evaluation) -- confirmed its tracked benchmark set for DeepSeek V4 Pro is SimpleQA, Apex Shortlist, Codeforces, Terminal-Bench 2.0, Toolathlon (none of which are MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval); the page's own "Accuracy Evals" filter panel returned "No data available for the current filters" when no specific benchmark was pre-selected, and no independent citation of the four target benchmarks surfaced via search. Searched ChinaTalk's DeepSeek V4 coverage (chinatalk.media/p/deepseek-v4) -- covers geopolitical/strategic narrative (Huawei chip migration, MIT license choice, market positioning vs ByteDance/Doubao), no benchmark figures at all. Directly fetched redreamality.com's "Mapping the DeepSeek V4 Evaluation Suite" guide (already used elsewhere in this corpus entry as a source for codeforces/terminal-bench-2) specifically asking whether it discusses MMLU/MATH/AIME/HumanEval -- it explicitly explains their absence as a deliberate, industry-wide phenomenon: "a year ago, a frontier report might cite MMLU, HumanEval, and GSM8K and call it a day. In 2026 that's not enough -- those three are saturated," and notes "Artificial Analysis removed MMLU-Pro, AIME 2025, and LiveCodeBench from Intelligence Index v4.0 in January precisely because frontier models had pushed them past the point where scores could discriminate." This corroborates, from an independent technical-analysis source, that the omission of these four legacy benchmarks from DeepSeek V4's own evaluation suite (and from third-party trackers following suit) is a deliberate, durable, industry-wide pattern rather than a reporting gap specific to this model or corpus entry.

Conclusion: All 4 of the 5 newly-specified source types were fully executable and returned negative results (Wayback Machine access was blocked by sandbox networking limitations across three different archive-access methods, so it contributes no evidence either way rather than a confirmed absence). Combined with the 2 prior rounds, this is now the third independent research pass -- covering official/independent trackers, HELM/arXiv/GitHub-eval-logs/regional/sibling-comparisons, and now Wayback/keynotes/investor-press/GitHub-discussions/technical-blogs -- to turn up zero credible, tier-matched (Think Max / Pro-Max) MMLU, MATH, AIME, or HumanEval scores for this model. The redreamality.com finding additionally supplies a plausible causal explanation (benchmark saturation leading labs and trackers to retire these metrics industry-wide in 2026) for why the absence is durable rather than an artifact of incomplete search. Re-affirmed as omitted_no_evidence for all four gaps; no existing entries altered.

2026-07-18 (mmmu-specific pass): Chased the flagged 'mmmu' gap per this round's instructions, which require checking capabilities/modalities fields first before searching further. This file's own modalities field is ["text"] only (no "vision"), and capabilities.vision is explicitly false -- confirmed by direct re-read of the corpus JSON (lines 14-25). Cross-checked against all 21 other DeepSeek-family corpus files in this directory: every DeepSeek-V4 variant (deepseek-v4-pro-non-think.json, deepseek-v4-pro-think-high.json, deepseek-v4-flash-non-think.json, deepseek-v4-flash-think-high.json, deepseek-v4-flash-think-max.json) likewise shows modalities: ["text"] and capabilities.vision: false, while DeepSeek maintains categorically separate, distinctly-named vision-language model families in this same corpus (deepseek-vl-7b.json, deepseek-vl2.json), both of which correctly show modalities: ["text", "vision"]. This confirms DeepSeek-V4-Pro (all reasoning tiers) is architecturally a text-only LLM with no vision encoder or multimodal input path -- a categorical distinction DeepSeek itself maintains via its separate VL product line, not merely an unreported capability. This file's own prior verificationNotes (2026-07-16 entry) independently reached the identical conclusion during the original mmlu/math/aime/humaneval/mmmu sweep ("MMMU additionally does not apply on capability grounds: this model's capabilities.vision is false (text-only), consistent with DeepSeek publishing no vision benchmark for any DeepSeek-V4 tier"), and no official DeepSeek-V4 launch material (HF model card, api-docs news post, arXiv:2606.19348 tech report) contains an MMMU row for any tier, consistent with a text-only checkpoint never being run against a vision-input benchmark. Per this round's explicit instruction to treat a confirmed non-vision capabilities field as sufficient grounds to stop rather than search further, MMMU is re-affirmed as omitted_no_evidence with reason "architecturally inapplicable (text-only model)" -- no score exists or could exist for this benchmark on this model, since MMMU requires image inputs this model cannot accept. No existing results were altered; no new entry added.
